presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: Sudden weakness in my right arm and leg
I can't speak properly and my face is drooping
Sudden facial drooping with arm weakness
Slurred speech and I can't move my right side

history_data.hpi: Patient was found by family members to have acute onset right-sided weakness and slurred speech approximately 85 minutes ago while sitting in an armchair. Last known well was 85 minutes prior when he was talking normally and moving all limbs. Family immediately called ambulance. Patient reports no warning signs or prodromal symptoms. Sudden onset of facial drooping, right arm and leg weakness, and difficulty forming words. No loss of consciousness. No headache.
patient_quotes: I can't speak the words properly.
My face feels numb and droopy.
I was completely fine a moment ago.
I feel very worried about this.
pmh_pool: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 8 years ago
Previous transient ischemic attack (TIA) — 2 years ago, resolved
medications_pool: Aspirin 81mg daily (for secondary prevention after TIA)

social_history.smoking: Former smoker — quit 5 years ago, smoked 40 pack-years
social_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol use — 2–3 drinks per week

cardiovascular: Heart rate irregular — atrial fibrillation noted
neurological: Alert, oriented to person and place (slightly confused regarding time)
Speech: expressive aphasia present, difficulty forming words, slurred
Right arm: pronator drift — unable to maintain arm elevation against gravity
Right leg: weakness 4/5 — able to move but with effort
Left side: normal strength and sensation
Cranial nerves: CN VII — right-sided palsy; other CNs intact
Gait: not tested due to acute weakness

investigations.Blood Glucose: 7.2 mmol/L — normal range. Hypoglycemia excluded as cause of neurological deficit.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Creatinine 92 µmol/L, Urea 6.8 mmol/L, eGFR 72 — all normal. Baseline kidney function acceptable for contrast administration.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 8.9 × 10⁹/L, Hb 13.6 g/dL, Platelets 245 × 10⁹/L — normal. No thrombocytopenia (which would contraindicate thrombolysis).
investigations.Coagulation Screen (PT/APPT/INR): PT 12 seconds, APPT 28 seconds, INR 1.0 — normal. Patient on apixaban (DOAC) but not warfarin; INR irrelevant but PT/APTT normal supports thrombolysis eligibility.
investigations.Lipid Profile: Total cholesterol 6.2 mmol/L, LDL 4.1 mmol/L (elevated), HDL 0.9 mmol/L (low), Triglycerides 2.1 mmol/L — abnormal lipid profile consistent with cardiovascular risk factors.
investigations.ECG: Atrial fibrillation with ventricular response 92 bpm. No acute ST changes. No evidence of acute MI. Confirms arrhythmia as potential embolic source.
investigations.Blood Group & Crossmatch: Group A positive. Crossmatch not required urgently but available if transfusion needed.
investigations.CT Head (Non-contrast): No acute intracranial haemorrhage. No mass effect. No hypodensity in middle cerebral artery territory — consistent with hyperacute ischemic stroke. No contraindication to thrombolysis identified on imaging.

hidden_diagnosis: Acute Ischemic Stroke (Left Middle Cerebral Artery Territory) — Within Thrombolysis Window

essential_questions: time of last known well — CRITICAL
anticoagulant use — dose and timing of last dose
last meal — relevant for aspiration risk
essential_examinations: NIHSS (National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale) — or equivalent comprehensive neurological assessment
critical_actions: Establish EXACT time of symptom onset or last known well
Stat non-contrast CT head to rule out haemorrhage
Stat blood glucose — hypoglycemia must be excluded
Obtain ECG to assess for atrial fibrillation
Rapid blood work: Urea & Electrolytes, FBC, Coagulation, Lipid profile
Establish IV access — two large-bore cannulae preferred
ASSESS THROMBOLYSIS ELIGIBILITY — time window is critical (4.5 hours for IV tPA; 24 hours for mechanical thrombectomy if no early signs of infarction)
If eligible and no contraindications: administer IV alteplase (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg)
NPO status until swallow assessment complete
red_flags: Facial droop — sign of brainstem or cortical involvement
Speech difficulty — expressive aphasia suggests dominant hemisphere involvement
Unilateral weakness — arm and leg together suggests cortical stroke
Time from onset — 85 minutes is WITHIN thrombolysis window
No loss of consciousness — suggests focused stroke, not global event
Multiple risk factors: age, hypertension, diabetes, AF, hyperlipidemia
acceptable_differentials: Transient ischemic attack (TIA) — but symptoms persistent, so less likely
Seizure with Todd's paralysis — no history of seizure activity
Brain tumour — wrong presentation, no time for progressive course
Migraine with aura — wrong age, wrong presentation
Subdural haematoma — no trauma history, CT negative
management_actions: Rapid assessment — aim to have patient in CT scanner within 10 minutes
Stat non-contrast CT head — rule out haemorrhage before thrombolysis
Stat blood tests: glucose, Urea & Electrolytes, FBC, Coagulation Screen, Lipid profile
Continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry
Blood pressure management: target SBP <180 mmHg before and during thrombolysis
IV tPA eligibility assessment — TIME FROM LAST KNOWN WELL IS CRITICAL
If eligible: IV alteplase (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg IV infusion over 60 minutes (10% bolus over 1 minute, remainder over 59 minutes)
Post-tPA monitoring: neurological checks every 15 minutes × 2 hours, then hourly × 6 hours; CT head 24 hours post-tPA
Prepare for mechanical thrombectomy if large vessel occlusion suspected
Antiplatelet therapy post-thrombolysis: aspirin 300mg day 1, then 75mg daily
Anticoagulation: continue apixaban after assessment period
critical_history_questions: When was the patient LAST KNOWN WELL? (Exact time)
When were symptoms FIRST NOTICED? (Exact time)
Who discovered the patient, and what exactly did they observe?
What were the initial symptoms — in what order did they occur?
Has the patient lost consciousness at any point?
Any focal weakness — which limbs, which side?
Any speech difficulty — can patient form words, understand speech?
Any facial drooping — which side?
Any vision changes or visual field loss?
Any headache or neck stiffness?
Any recent seizure activity?
Is patient on anticoagulation? What drug and dose? When was last dose?
Is patient on antiplatelet therapy?
Recent bleeding or surgery?
Recent blood pressure readings — baseline control?
Blood sugar control — any recent hypoglycaemic episodes?
Known atrial fibrillation?
Previous stroke or TIA — when, what treatment?
Last meal — time and type of food?
Any recent head injury or trauma?
Currently smoking or recent smoking?
